---
pagetitle: "Chapter 2: An Introduction to R"
engine: knitr
format:
  live-revealjs:
    theme: simple
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    toc: false
    chalkboard: true
    footer: "STA258 | Chapter 2"
    pdf-separate-fragments: false
    html-math-method: mathjax
    code-copy: true
    code-fold: false
    webr:
      packages:
        - ggplot2
    live:
      show-solutions: true
---

# Chapter 2: An Introduction to R {.title-slide}

<style>
.reveal {
  font-family: Arial, sans-serif;
  color: #2f3437;
}

.reveal h1,
.reveal h2,
.reveal h3 {
  color: #2f3437;
}

.reveal h2 {
  border-bottom: 3px solid #00695c;
  padding-bottom: 8px;
  margin-bottom: 20px;
}

.title-slide {
  text-align: center;
}

.defbox {
  background: #c8e6f3;
  padding: 18px;
  border-left: 7px solid #00695c;
  border-radius: 10px;
  font-size: 0.88em;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.examplebox {
  background: #eeeeee;
  padding: 18px;
  border-radius: 10px;
  font-size: 0.88em;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.thinkbox {
  background: #fff8e1;
  padding: 18px;
  border-left: 7px solid #f9a825;
  border-radius: 10px;
  font-size: 0.88em;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.codebox {
  background: #f5f5f5;
  padding: 14px;
  border-radius: 8px;
  font-size: 0.82em;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.big {
  font-size: 1.2em;
  font-weight: bold;
  color: #00695c;
}

.center {
  text-align: center;
}

.small-text {
  font-size: 0.72em;
}

.tight li {
  margin-bottom: 0.25em;
}
</style>

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

STA258: Statistics with Applied Probability  
University of Toronto Mississauga

![](cat.png){width="300"}

::: notes
This chapter introduces R, RStudio, and the first basic programming ideas students need for the course.
:::

---

## Roadmap

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Setup and Purpose

- [2.1 The Statistical Computing Language](#statistical-computing-language)
- [2.2 Installing R and RStudio](#installing-r-and-rstudio)
- [2.2.3 Positron](#positron)
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Programming Basics

- [2.3 Programming in R](#programming-in-r)
- [R as a Calculator](#r-as-calculator)
- [Data Structures](#data-structures)
- [Functions, Histograms, and Packages](#functions-histograms-packages)
:::
:::::

---

## 2.1 The Statistical Computing Language {#statistical-computing-language}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>R</strong><br>
R is a programming language used for data manipulation, statistics, graphics, and reproducible analysis.
</div>

<br>

R is useful because it can:

- perform calculations on numbers, vectors, arrays, and matrices
- generate high-quality graphs
- use built-in functions and user-contributed packages
- save analysis steps in scripts that can be reused later

---

## Why Use R?

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Point-and-click software</strong><br>
Often easier at first, but steps may be harder to document and repeat.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>R scripts</strong><br>
Each step is written down, so the analysis is easier to check, repeat, and modify.
</div>
:::
:::::

<br>

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Main idea:</strong><br>
R makes statistical work reproducible, transparent, and easier to automate.
</div>

---

## 2.2 Installing R and RStudio {#installing-r-and-rstudio}

Before working with R code, students need two things:

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>R</strong><br>
The programming language that performs the calculations.
</div>
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
<div class="defbox">
<strong>RStudio</strong><br>
The interface used to write scripts, run code, view files, and inspect output.
</div>
:::
:::::

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
Think of R as the engine, and RStudio as the dashboard used to control it.
</div>

---

## 2.2.1 Installing R

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Step 1</strong><br>
Go to the official R project website: <code>cran.r-project.org</code>
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox tight">
<strong>Choose your operating system:</strong>
<ul>
<li><strong>Windows:</strong> Download R for Windows → base → download the installer</li>
<li><strong>macOS:</strong> Download R for macOS → choose the correct <code>.pkg</code> file</li>
<li><strong>Linux:</strong> Follow the distribution-specific instructions</li>
</ul>
</div>

<br>

After installation, R can be accessed from a terminal or through RStudio.

---

## Installing R: Teaching Note

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Important:</strong><br>
Students should install R <em>before</em> installing RStudio, because RStudio needs an R installation to run code.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
The ebook screenshot shows the CRAN page where students choose their operating system and download the correct installer.
</div>

---

## 2.2.2 Installing RStudio

<div class="defbox">
<strong>RStudio</strong><br>
RStudio is an integrated development environment designed for working with R.
</div>

<br>

It provides:

- a script editor
- an R console
- an environment and history viewer
- a file browser
- plots, packages, help, viewer, and presentation panes

---

## RStudio Workflow

<div class="examplebox tight">
<strong>Basic workflow:</strong>
<ol>
<li>Open RStudio.</li>
<li>Create a new script.</li>
<li>Write code in the Source pane.</li>
<li>Run the code.</li>
<li>Read the output in the Console or Plots pane.</li>
</ol>
</div>

<br>

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Shortcut:</strong><br>
Use <code>Cmd + Enter</code> (Mac) or <code>Ctrl + Enter</code> (Windows) to run the current line of code.
</div>

---

## The Four Main RStudio Panes

| Pane | Purpose |
|:---|:---|
| Source | Write and edit scripts or Quarto files |
| Console | Run commands and view immediate output |
| Environment / History | View saved objects and past commands |
| Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer | Manage files, plots, packages, and documentation |

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
The ebook shows the RStudio home screen and the layout after opening a new script.
</div>

---

## 2.2.3 Positron {#positron}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Positron</strong><br>
Positron is another desktop environment from Posit for data science and statistical computing.
</div>

<br>

For this course, students mainly need **RStudio**.

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
Positron may be useful in future workflows, but it is not required for STA258.
</div>

---

## 2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R {#programming-in-r}

R is especially useful for statistics because it is designed for:

- data analysis
- statistical calculations
- visualization
- reproducible reporting

<br>

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Course goal:</strong><br>
Students do not need to become software engineers. They need enough R to calculate, summarize, visualize, and report data clearly.
</div>

---

## 2.3.1 R as a Powerful Calculator {#r-as-calculator}

<div class="defbox">
<strong>R can be used like a calculator.</strong><br>
It supports arithmetic, exponentiation, logarithms, trigonometric functions, and summary calculations.
</div>

<br>

| Operation | R Code | Result |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Addition | `2 + 3` | 5 |
| Subtraction | `4 - 1` | 3 |
| Multiplication | `6 * 2` | 12 |
| Division | `8 / 2` | 4 |
| Exponentiation | `5^2` | 25 |

---

## Try It: Basic Arithmetic

Edit the numbers and run the code.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

2 + 3
4 - 1
6 * 2
8 / 2
5^2
```

---

## More Arithmetic Operators

| Operation | R Code | Meaning |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Modulo | `9 %% 4` | Remainder after division |
| Integer division | `9 %/% 4` | Whole-number part of division |

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

9 %% 4
9 %/% 4
```

---

## Vector Operations

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Vector</strong><br>
A vector is a one-dimensional collection of values of the same type.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

x <- c(1, 2, 3)
y <- c(4, 5, 6)

x + y
x * y
x^2
```

<br>

<div class="small-text">
Operations on vectors are performed element by element.
</div>

---

## Math Functions in R

R has many built-in mathematical functions.

| Function | Meaning | Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `sqrt()` | Square root | `sqrt(16)` |
| `log()` | Natural log | `log(10)` |
| `log10()` | Log base 10 | `log10(100)` |
| `exp()` | Exponential function | `exp(1)` |
| `abs()` | Absolute value | `abs(-5)` |
| `round()` | Round to decimal places | `round(3.1415, 2)` |

---

## Try It: Math Functions

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

sqrt(16)
log(10)
log10(100)
exp(1)
abs(-5)
round(3.1415, 2)
sin(pi / 2)
cos(0)
tan(pi / 4)
```

---

## Summary Functions

Common functions for summarizing data:

| Function | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| `mean(x)` | Average |
| `sum(x)` | Total |
| `min(x)` | Minimum |
| `max(x)` | Maximum |
| `sd(x)` | Standard deviation |
| `var(x)` | Variance |

---

## Try It: Summary Functions

Change the values in `x`, then rerun the code.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

x <- c(10, 3, 8, 25, 7)

mean(x)
sum(x)
min(x)
max(x)
sd(x)
var(x)
```

---

## Quick Practice: Calculate the Mean

Fill in the blank to calculate the mean.

```{webr}
#| exercise: mean-practice

x <- c(10, 3, 8, 25, 7)
____(x)
```

::: {.solution exercise="mean-practice"}
::: {.callout-tip title="Solution"}
```r
mean(x)
```
:::
:::

---

## 2.3.2 Basic R Data Structures {#data-structures}

The primary data structures introduced in the ebook are:

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
- Vectors
- Lists
- Matrices
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
- Arrays
- Factors
- Data frames
:::
:::::

<br>

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Main idea:</strong><br>
Different data structures organize information in different ways.
</div>

---

## Vectors

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Vector</strong><br>
A one-dimensional sequence of values of the same type.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

v <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
v
```

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
Vectors are the basic building blocks for many R operations.
</div>

---

## Lists

<div class="defbox">
<strong>List</strong><br>
A flexible structure that can store elements of different types and sizes.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

my_list <- list(
  name   = "Alice",
  age    = 25,
  scores = c(90, 85, 88),
  passed = TRUE
)

my_list$name
my_list[[2]]
my_list[["scores"]]
```

---

## Matrices

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Matrix</strong><br>
A two-dimensional data structure with rows and columns. All elements must be the same type.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

m <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), nrow = 3, ncol = 2)
m

m[1, 2]
m[, 1]
m[2, ]
```

---

## Matrix Operations

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

m <- matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6), nrow = 3, ncol = 2)

t(m)
m %*% t(m)
```

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
The operator <code>%*%</code> is used for matrix multiplication. <code>t()</code> transposes a matrix.
</div>

---

## Arrays

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Array</strong><br>
A multi-dimensional structure that can have more than two dimensions.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

a <- array(1:12, dim = c(2, 3, 2))
a

a[1, 2, 1]
a[, , 2]
```

---

## Factors

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Factor</strong><br>
A data structure used to represent categorical variables.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

gender <- factor(c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male"))
gender
levels(gender)
```

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
Factors are useful for categorical data such as gender, education level, or survey responses.
</div>

---

## Data Frames

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Data frame</strong><br>
A table-like structure where columns are variables and rows are observations.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

df <- data.frame(
  name   = c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"),
  age    = c(25, 30, 28),
  passed = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE)
)

df
df$name
df[1, ]
df[, 2]
```

---

## Data Structure Summary

| Structure | Shape | Best Used For |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Vector | One dimension | Values of the same type |
| List | Flexible | Mixed types or grouped information |
| Matrix | Two dimensions | Same-type numerical tables |
| Array | Multiple dimensions | Higher-dimensional data |
| Factor | One dimension | Categorical variables |
| Data frame | Rows and columns | Real datasets |

---

## 2.3.3 Using Basic Functions in R {#functions-histograms-packages}

R has many built-in functions that help calculate, transform, and summarize data.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

x <- c(10, 3, 8, 25, 7)

max(x)
min(x)
sort(x)
sort(x, decreasing = TRUE)
order(x)
```

---

## Function Logic

<div class="defbox">
<strong>General form</strong><br>
Most R functions use the structure <code>function_name(input)</code>.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
For example, <code>mean(x)</code> tells R to apply the function <code>mean</code> to the object <code>x</code>.
</div>

<br>

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Common mistake:</strong><br>
Forgetting brackets, commas, or quotation marks will cause R to return an error.
</div>

---

## 2.3.4 Make a Histogram Using RStudio

The ebook uses the built-in `faithful` dataset to demonstrate a histogram.

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Histogram</strong><br>
A graph that shows the distribution of a numerical variable by grouping values into intervals called bins.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

names(faithful)
```

---

## Try It: Create a Histogram

Run the code to create a histogram of waiting times in the `faithful` dataset.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

hist(faithful$waiting)
```

---

## Histogram Breaks and Counts

The ebook also shows how to access the numerical values behind a histogram.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

hist(faithful$waiting, plot = FALSE)$breaks
hist(faithful$waiting, plot = FALSE)$counts
```

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
<strong>breaks</strong> show the interval endpoints. <strong>counts</strong> show how many observations fall in each interval.
</div>

---

## Quick Practice: Histogram Variable

Change the code so it creates a histogram of `eruptions` instead of `waiting`.

```{webr}
#| exercise: histogram-practice

hist(faithful$waiting)
```

::: {.solution exercise="histogram-practice"}
::: {.callout-tip title="Solution"}
```r
hist(faithful$eruptions)
```
:::
:::

---

## 2.3.5 Installing and Loading Packages

<div class="defbox">
<strong>Package</strong><br>
A package is a collection of extra functions, data, and tools that extend what R can do.
</div>

<br>

<div class="examplebox">
<strong>Install a package once:</strong>
<pre><code>install.packages("ggplot2")</code></pre>

<strong>Load it every new R session:</strong>
<pre><code>library(ggplot2)</code></pre>
</div>

---

## Package Reminder

<div class="thinkbox">
<strong>Important:</strong><br>
You install a package only once, but you must load it with <code>library()</code> every time you restart RStudio.
</div>

<br>

```{webr}
#| autorun: false

installed.packages()[1:5, 1]
help.search("regression")
```

---

## Chapter 2 Summary

- R is used for statistics, graphics, data manipulation, and reproducible analysis
- RStudio is the main interface for writing and running R code
- R can be used as a calculator for arithmetic and mathematical functions
- Vectors, lists, matrices, arrays, factors, and data frames each store data differently
- Functions take inputs and produce outputs using the form `function_name(input)`
- Histograms display the distribution of a numerical variable
- Packages extend R's functionality and must be loaded each session with `library()`

---

## End of Chapter 2

<div class="center">
<br>
<span class="big">End of Chapter 2</span>
<br><br>
<a href="test.html">← Back to Home</a>
</div>

::: notes
Update the link if the landing page file name changes.
:::
